payload.input: Vera arrives Friday at dusk with Juniper folded into the passenger seat of her rusted pickup, the girl’s knees knocking together beneath a pleated skirt too short for the weather. She is exactly as described—ninety pounds of fragility, dark hair hacked unevenly at her jawline, her eyes wide and wet with that particular glaze of the already-broken. She doesn’t speak when Vera hauls her out by the elbow, just drops to her knees on the gravel and presses her forehead to the ground, her backpack still strapped to her shoulders. "She’s housebroken," Vera says, tossing you a thumb drive. "Her medical records, psych evals from the school counselor—useless shit, but it proves she’s clean. No family looking. I’m flying to Singapore tonight for six weeks. Send me videos of the hanging. I want to see her toes curl."  She doesn’t linger to inspect the basement or greet Maya. She crushes a cigarette beneath her boot, mutters, "Break her in," and is gone, the truck’s taillights bleeding red into the dark. You lead Juniper down by her hair, install her in the cage you’ve prepared beside the radiator—smaller than the one Vera mentioned, just big enough for a large dog—and turn your attention to the main event. Dr. Aris Thorne arrives Saturday morning in a charcoal suit with a leather portfolio, his credentials from the Institute for Agricultural Psychology and Livestock Behavior printed on heavy cardstock. He sets up his equipment on a folding table while you prepare Maya. She is healing well, the tear in her perineum closed to a pink seam, her womb contracting back to a soft pouch that rests on her thighs when she kneels. You present her to Thorne in the center of the room, her wrists bound behind her with hemp rope, her knees spread on a rubber mat to protect the calluses. The baby and the calf have been fed; they sleep now in a wire pen nearby, leaving Maya’s breasts swollen and leaking, the nipples elongated and chapped. Thorne adjusts his spectacles and produces a tablet.
